speaker: interviewer
text: And the date is December 7th, 1995. Kurt, we left off on the last tape with you mentioning that you were now going into a town that was going to surrender. You were with three tanks moving in. Before we go on with that story, I want to backtrack one minute to ask you, when you were in France, you were interrogating some prisoners of war. Did anything unusual occur?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember one morning, we were quartered in a-- in a very large building. You might almost call it a mansion. We would often find quarters like that. And it had a courtyard in which our mess had been set up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came down for breakfast. And I see that on the other side of the courtyard, somebody is walking toward me, a big, burly man, who could-- could be an officer, and surrounded by-- by a group of people who turned out to be liberated [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] soldiers of other armies, Russians, French, Poles. And they had-- they were carrying guns of every conceivable make that they had apparently captured. And they had three German prisoners in tow.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this man walked up to me and said, here, I got a few Krauts for you. They're-- they're awfully glad to be-- that the war is over for them, and you won't have any trouble with them. And so I would go through my normal routine, because I always had to establish who had captured them and where they had been captured, because that had some bearing on-- you know, on the lines that we were trying to determine where some of these people were.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- so this was-- I was stationed [INAUDIBLE] in a town called Maintenon, and-- about 20, 25 miles outside of Paris. And I got to the point of where I said, and where did you capture those? And he said, well, they were on the railroad tracks in Rambouillet. And-- but they didn't put up much of a fight. And I could see, here was this-- this conglomerate of people, a soldier from every army and all these weapons, strange weapons. And I couldn't quite make out who had captured them. So I said, and who shall I say captured these Germans? He said, just tell them Hemingway. I said, you mean the Hemingway? He said, yup. And so he-- he said, well, but these Krauts won't give you any problem. That was one of the more amusing things that happened in the course of this.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- as I later on found out and also read about, he-- he was in fact involved in-- in the liberation of Paris, but also in this-- in this town of Rambouillet. I found one of his letters in which he-- he describes this episode. And he would then-- he, of course, was not supposed to-- he was only supposed to report news, not make the news. And he was reprimanded for that, in fact, later. But he would always expose himself to the greatest dangers. He later on stayed with our division for a short while, would go out with-- with the engineers on mine-sweeping missions. And he-- he couldn't find enough danger to suit him. And he had surrounded himself with all those liberated prisoners of Allied armies who then went out with him in search of Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now we're going to move forward again to the town that you were in that was going to surrender. What was the name of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Volary. It-- it also had a German name, Wallern, which it was known by in those days. Today and in Czech, it's called Volary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so despite the shelling, we made our way into town. And we each went about our business. And I-- by asking around, I found out where the field hospital was that the Germans had set up. And in it, I did find some-- some German wounded, and I made some arrangements so that we could evacuate them later. And this was late in the day. And because-- because it was getting dark and the Germans were still offering resistance and the war wasn't over yet-- this was the 6th of May-- we decided to go back to our headquarters and to come back the following morning. And there was another reason for that, because when I got back and I had a chance to exchange some information with the military government officer, I found out that the mayor of the town had told them that in that town, there had been-- there were a group of Polish and Hungarian Jewish young women who had been on a death march for a long time and whose SS guards had locked them into a vacant factory building. And when the guard saw the handwriting on the wall, they still wanted to destroy them and put dynamite charges on the factory building, which fortunately didn't go off because of the rains. And then the guards put on civilian clothes and disappeared. So we knew we had to do-- and that they were in deplorable condition.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we knew we had to do something, had to come back with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] great medical reinforcements the following morning. And that's precisely what we did. This was now May 7th, and we went back with the medics. And the factory building was pointed out to me. And I approached it by jeep and got out, saw these walking skeletons scurrying back and forth to-- to a water pump that was in the center of the yard in front of the factory. And and they-- I mean, they-- I had never seen anything as bad as that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And over on the other side near the-- of the yard, near the entrance to the building, I saw one young woman standing, and I decided to approach her. And I went up to her and asked her about her companions and how they all were, and we exchanged some words in German. And-- and I asked her, you know, whether-- where they had been. And she told me that they had been on a death march since-- more-- with some stops since January of that year. And I asked her to take me inside and let me see the others. And so we went inside. And of course, it's nearly impossible to describe the scene that I found inside, with all these women scattered on the floor in-- in various stages of illness, and some of them obviously quite close to death, and in rags and without blankets or-- quite obviously without food. And it-- it was something that one never, never can forget. And even I, who had steeled myself, knowing-- having known by that time the extent of the-- of the German Nazi mentality, knew that I should steel myself against anything we might find. But even that was simply too overwhelming. And the most unforgettable and remarkable thing that happened then was that this young woman, who had shown me inside, made a sweeping gesture over this whole scene and said the following words, 'Noble be man, merciful and good.' And it was hard for me to believe that she, in that condition, under those circumstances, had been able to summon a line from a poem by the German poet Goethe, a poem called 'The Divine,' because by doing so, she chose to underscore the grim irony of what I was seeing there more effectively than anything else that she might have said. I talked to some of those women. One of them, it was pointed out to me later, was a former university professor from Prague, still addressed me in English. And when I tried to say some words of comfort, she said, oh, I'm sorry. It's too late, too late for me. And I found out that she died later that day. I also found a young girl there, still very pretty but also in terrible condition, who was at that time probably only 15 years old, although the Germans didn't know it, because her sisters had sort of protected her and nursed her along, because she would have been selected as to go among the children who-- who were destined for death if she had given her true age. But she was 15 years old, and despite the fact that she actually still looked pretty, she went to the hospital as did all the others-- of course, our medics took them immediately to the hospital-- and was there for a few months and still died later that year from the-- what she had gone through on the march.

speaker: interviewer
text: So since our medics, you know, took them to that field hospital I had seen the day before, I-- I knew that I wanted to go back there. But because this was May 7th and the next day was the official end of the war, I-- I was really too busy with theses masses of surrendering German troops to get away. And it took me a few days to get back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I did get back, I wanted to know, of course, how they were all doing. But I especially remember this woman that had left this impression on my mind, and-- and I wanted to find her. And I finally did find her bunk. And I learned that she had collapsed once she was admitted to the hospital and was now listed in critical condition. But she was still quite lucid, and we had a talk. And from that time on, I simply needed to find out more about her and how she would fare. And so I made it my business to visit all of them, but all this gravitated towards this one young woman, and saw her through a real crisis yet at a later date. And eventually, she did improve. And the more we talked and the more we found out about each other, we-- we were struck by the similarity of backgrounds and found the many things that we liked about each other, the similar, but also that we liked-- had similar likes and dislikes. And out of that grew the relationship which led to our marriage a year later in Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name at that time was Gerda Weissmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you came upon the factory and you saw the condition of the people, were there many dead bodies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Some of them had already died. And some of them were to die in days to come. And our medics, you also have to understand-- our doctors, rather, had been trained to deal with battle casualties. But they had never seen anything like that, and in many instances had to improvise as to how to-- how to nurse these women back to health, because if you allowed them to eat too much, they could die from that. And and-- you know, many of their limbs had to be amputated, and to make decisions what-- when to amputate and when not to amputate and how much you allow them to eat. I once spoke to one of the doctors who was there-- this happened not so many years ago. And he wrote us a letter that they had to go strictly by instinct and improvise, because they had not been trained for that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So that out of 120 young women who were still liberated, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think more than 30 died yet after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a burial detail?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we also discovered a-- a mass grave outside the town, where-- where in the last days, the SS had still shot a number of-- of the girls on the death march, some of them because they tried to get away and were caught. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we made the German population of the town disinter them and give them a decent burial. And the-- there are films available of-- of that burial in the town. We made the whole population of the town march past the bodies as they were laid out before the-- before burial. And at the funeral, our divisional chaplain read-- who also spoke German still read a eulogy, which actually was something that Gerda Weissmann wrote at that time, which was an ode to the American liberators, an ode of thanks for liberating them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reaction of the local townspeople?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, most of them, of course, pretended that they had never heard of-- of such conditions and didn't know that this type of thing existed, as was the reaction in almost any other part of Germany. To the people who live nearby, even the people who in the death camps could smell it in the air, they all pretended they didn't really know what was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- you mentioned there was a chaplain present?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] also, he-- you know, he recited the Jewish prayers and the kaddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: The actual condition of the factory that you found the girls in, what was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: The factory?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the factory was actually in bad repair, because it hadn't been used in some time. It was-- it was at one time used to make bicycle trains, and-- but hadn't been used in a number of years so that it had deteriorated. And when we went back four years ago, we still found the same places and the same halls and the same rooms. And-- and they-- they hadn't deteriorated much worse than they were in those days, although they were slightly better in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you capture and interrogate any SS from this factory, or were they all gone by the time--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They were all gone by that time. And-- so we-- no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your job at this time was really to help the-- the women that had been--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, and see if we couldn't nurse them back to health.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? You went to visit Gerda you said almost every day, and you were still attached to this same unit.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Well, I-- I stayed in the area for, oh, perhaps a couple of weeks, during which time I-- I visited as often as I could. And when-- then, of course, after the war, at that time, it was already clear that this area would ultimately be occupied by Russian troops. And although when our division was pulled out of there and I was reassigned to an entirely different unit because the war was now over, we knew that the division that would come in after us would also have to leave, so I befriended a captain from that division and made him promise that when the time came that the American troops would have to withdraw because the Russian troops would take over, to make it possible for Gerda and her friends to be evacuated to the American zone, which he promised and which in fact he-- he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that-- I, in the meantime, had been reassigned to an intelligence center in-- in Bavaria, where I had some new duties. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it happened that one evening, I found out that Gerda and a friend of hers had in fact found me because I was in correspondence with this captain, had found me and had come to the town. And I-- I found them a place to stay temporarily and then found them a place in Munich where they could stay more permanently.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from then on, I would visit much more often, because now it was possible, although I-- I still ran into some things that now seem funny but weren't so funny at the time. And that is, because there was a ban on fraternization with Germans, meant to keep our GIs from taking up with-- with German girls, I had a difficult time to visit Gerda, because I would take my Jeep-- this was, you know, quite a distance outside of Munich-- take my Jeep and-- and go to the house I had found for-- for the two of them, and then couldn't leave it outside the house, because the-- the MPs that were roaming the city would have found the-- the Jeep and thought that here's somebody who is trying to fraternize with Germans, so that I always had to find a way of hiding the Jeep when I wanted to visit. Funny now, but at the time, it presented a problem. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And from then on, our relationship developed even closer, and-- until [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] September, when-- at which point I had enough points to-- to be sent home. And I suddenly got orders to get ready to be discharged from the service to be sent home, discharged from the service. Now, at that point, Gerda and I got engaged. This was September 13th.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there were no consulates in Germany that would have allowed her to leave also. And at any rate, the-- the whole situation was so-- in such a state of flux, where you didn't know-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you didn't know when she could come exactly. But also I didn't know where I would settle down once I was back in the States. I didn't know would I stay in Buffalo or would I go to New York City, where both my sister and my brother lived by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we decided that it would be best if I-- if I go back, determine all those things, and find a job so that we could get married, thinking that, of course, fiancees of servicemen would-- would be allowed much sooner than others to-- to join them. But as it turned out, we ran into just one obstacle after another. And-- and no consulates opened up in Germany for the next eight, nine months, so that we finally got to a point-- and Gerda first got to a point where she felt that she would not get out of Germany unless she found some other way, and heard through friends that if she could get into France, that she could find a consulate there, an American consulate, and-- which she did. And when-- still absolutely-- it still took her-- there was just so much red tape involved that we decided-- I finally decided that the best way to solve this matter is if I went back this time to Paris, we would get married, and then Gerda could come. And that's what I did, not without many, many obstacles on both sides. And so we got married in June of '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned that Gerda had passed through another big crisis that you helped her through. When did that occur?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it-- it occurred probably a week or 10 days after liberation. Perhaps Gerda can remember the-- the time better than I can, but it was no more than that, in which they had sent her to an isolation ward in the hospital on another floor, where they sent people they expected to die. And it just so happens by this time, you know, I wasn't always sure of being able to see her every day. I think this may already have been after I moved out of the area, so that it would have perhaps been a few weeks after liberation. And I just happened to come that evening. And when the doctors in fact had given up hope, I came. And apparently that got her to-- gave her some sort of a boost in her morale, and-- and she pulled through.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mentioned also that you were assigned new duties after you left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were those new duties?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were, of course, to-- to interrogate-- first of all, to round up any known high-level Nazis or possibly-- and/or war criminals and to-- to find them. And we kept a-- a stockade in that intelligence center, where-- where we had found already a number of collaborationists from other countries who had collaborated with the Germans, some of them pretty high level, and some of the-- some of their war criminals, some-- some others who had been with the-- with special German commando units that-- I remember one of them, his name was Skorzeny. He had engineered the kidnapping of Mussolini-- I think it may have been away from partisans at the time-- and who had-- who had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] done some of those major feats that were accomplished during that time. And he himself had been imprisoned several times, each time escaping, and in fact escaped from-- from our enclosure as well, never to be heard from again, to my knowledge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this the same place where you interrogated Hitler's chauffeur?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there anybody else that was infamous during this period of time that you interrogated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the name today doesn't mean too much, but there was a Yugoslav collaborationist by name of Tiso. He was a high-level government official who was found-- found to be guilty of war crimes and was later hanged. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you came to America, back to America, when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came back in October.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were married already.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not married.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you went back to Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the whole idea was for me to find a place to live, find a job, and establish something that would allow us to get married. So I did find a job. I was actually separated from the service in December, because between the time I arrived in-- in October, I went to Fort Dix, which was a separation center. But I-- I had to stay there for a number of weeks. And by the time I was, in fact, discharged, it was December.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the rank that you had when you were discharged?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was a second lieutenant at that time.
